article.title: US defense official overseeing AI reaped millions selling xAI stock

summary: Emil Michael, the Pentagon’s under secretary for research and engineering, profited up to $24 million by selling stock in Elon Musk’s private AI company xAI shortly after overseeing Pentagon agreements with the firm. This raises significant ethical concerns given his role in AI procurement and conflict of interest laws prohibiting officials from benefiting financially from government decisions.
keyPoints: Emil Michael sold xAI shares on January 9, 2026, for $5 million to $25 million, up from a maximum $1 million value at his Pentagon appointment in May 2025.
Michael oversaw Pentagon AI initiatives and negotiations, including two contracts with xAI announced in July and December 2025.
He received a divestiture certificate on December 18, 2025, requiring sale of xAI stock to avoid conflicts of interest; sale occurred 22 days later.
xAI is a private company behind Musk’s Grok chatbot, not publicly traded, with unclear share acquisition and sale details.
Ethics experts warn that holding AI stock while managing related government contracts likely violates criminal conflict of interest statutes.
Pentagon spokesperson emphasized existing ethics frameworks but declined detailed comment on Michael’s transactions.

rewrite.title: Pentagon AI Chief Profits Millions from Musk’s xAI Stock
rewrite.body:
Emil Michael, the Pentagon’s under secretary for research and engineering since May 2025, realized a substantial financial gain by selling his stake in Elon Musk’s private AI company xAI earlier this year. Government ethics disclosures reveal Michael sold his shares on January 9, 2026, for between $5 million and $25 million, a remarkable increase from the estimated $1 million value when he joined the department. This represents a gain ranging from 400% to nearly 4,800%. 

Michael’s role involves overseeing AI acquisitions and partnerships, including two Pentagon agreements with xAI announced in July and December 2025. Notably, he received a divestiture certificate on December 18, 2025, mandating the sale of his xAI holdings to comply with conflict of interest regulations, with the sale finalized just days later. Despite these measures, the timing and scale of his financial benefit raise serious ethical questions. Experts highlight that government officials are legally barred from participating in decisions that could enrich their private interests, suggesting Michael’s continued stock ownership during contract negotiations may breach federal law.

xAI, known for its Grok chatbot, remains a private entity with opaque valuation and share transfer processes, complicating transparency. The Pentagon, now styling itself as the Department of War, has aggressively promoted AI integration across its workforce of three million military and civilian personnel. Michael’s tenure has been marked by public disputes with AI contractors and a highly visible push for AI adoption, endorsed by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth. While the Pentagon asserts it maintains rigorous ethics oversight, the case underscores the challenges of managing conflicts in rapidly evolving tech procurement amid close ties between officials and industry leaders.

This episode highlights the broader risks of potential conflicts of interest in government AI procurement, especially as private firms like xAI gain strategic military contracts. It also raises questions about the adequacy of current ethics frameworks in preventing officials from profiting from their oversight roles in emerging technologies critical to national security.
